summary: DHH reflects on creative downturns — periods where productivity stalls after intense creative highs or disruptions to routine. His remedy is simply to wait it out, filling the time with menial tasks until boredom itself reignites the creative spark. He describes creativity as cyclical, following natural patterns of intensity and dormancy. With age, he's learned to accept these oscillations rather than fighting them.
key_points: Creative downturns are best escaped not by forcing productivity but by waiting them out while handling mundane tasks
Boredom with distractions is the mechanism that restarts creativity — when nothing else is appealing, the editor beckons
Downturns typically follow dopamine highs from intense creative periods, or prolonged breaks from routine
Accepting the cyclical nature of creativity becomes easier with age and experience
money_quotes: If I'm stuck in a creative downturn, there's usually only one remedy: keep going.
The curse is usually broken when I'm sufficiently bored with the menial, the managerial, and the internet.
When there is literally nothing better to do than open the editor and code.
As if the creative juices really are juices, and you can chug the jug.
It can't be eternal sunshine in the productive mind.
The grey, wet days of today soak the soil for the sun of tomorrow.
Don't argue with nature. Just wait. It'll be bright before you know it.
key_insight: Creative downturns aren't problems to solve but natural cycles to endure — boredom with distractions is itself the mechanism that restores the urge to create.
